text: The surgeon uses a transperitoneal approach to mobilize the ascending colon, exposing Girota's fascia. Dissection begins at the inferior aspect and progresses cranially using both sharp and blunt techniques. The duodenum is identified and retracted medially, revealing the inferior vena cava.

The surgeon dissects the lateral edge of the inferior vena cava, exposing more of its length and the posterior abdominal wall. The ureter and gonadal vessels are retracted anteriorly, revealing the psoas muscle. An additional retracting arm is inserted to aid dissection towards the renal hilum. The renal vein is exposed using sharp and blunt dissection, followed by identification of a single renal artery, visible as a white vertical structure. The renal artery is exposed sufficiently for hilar clamping during the excision of the renal lesion.

The surgeon completes hyalodissection and exposes the renal lesion by incising Girota's fascia and clearing perinephric fat. The upper pole of the kidney with the tumor is visible, surrounded by retained perinephric fat. An intraoperative ultrasound is used to determine the dimensions of the renal lesion and mark the excision line. Hilar clamping is performed, starting with the renal artery followed by the renal vein, with warm ischemia time maintained at around ten minutes.

The surgeon excises the renal lesion using traction and counter traction to identify the dissection plane. The sucker deflects the renal cortex inferiorly, while bipolar forceps elevate the lesion. After removal, rhinoraphy is performed with a hemostatic suture running along the base of the excision defect to ensure hemostasis and close any inadvertent entry into the collecting system.

The surgeon withdraws the suture through the renal cortex and controls it using a Hemilox suture, applying tension to enhance hemostasis. The defect is then closed with a V-lock suture, completing the closure process.

The surgeon removes the hilar clamping, first releasing the renal clamp followed by the renal artery clamp, after a warm ischemia time of eight and a half minutes. Compression sutures are applied to ensure hemostasis, and a second Hemolock clip is secured to prevent slipping of the previous clip and V-lock suture. The excised specimen is placed in a retrieval bag. Flow seal is applied to the edge of the defect, and Girota's fascia is closed over the surgical site.
